content.full_text:
July 8, 1991


John R. Calhoun
Long Beach City Attorney
Office of City Attorney
City Hall
333 West Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach, CA 90802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-298

Dear Mr. Calhoun:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Long Beach City Councilmember Douglas S. Drummond
regarding the councilmember's responsibilities under the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Councilmember Drummond participate in Long Beach City
Council consideration of a revision to the transportation element
of the city's general plan which would include a grade separation
project slated for an intersection within 300 feet of property
owned and leased out by Councilmember Drummond?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Drummond may not participate in any decisions
concerning the grade separation project if the decision will have
any financial effect on the fair market or rental value of the
real property of the councilmember. The councilmember will also
have to disqualify with respect to all decisions concerning the
revision of the transportation element of the city's general plan
which are critical to the project as a whole and could result in
termination or substantial modification of the grade separation
project. However, Councilmember Drummond may participate in

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 2


considering other components of the revision of the transportation
element of the city's general plan which will not affect the grade
separation project, provided the councilmember has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.

                              FACTS

     The Long Beach City Council will be considering a
comprehensive revision of the transportation element of the city's
general plan.  The proposed revised element applies traffic
circulation within the entire city and includes over 830 miles of
streets and approximately 9,000 intersections.  The City of Long
Beach has a population of approximately 430,000 persons and covers
a geographic area of roughly 50 square miles.

     In the revision of the element, the intersection of Ocean
Boulevard and Alamitos Avenue has been slated for a grade
separation project.  You stated the project would reconstruct the
streets to intersect on two different levels.  You also stated the
project may require the purchase of real property around the
intersection.

     Councilmember Drummond owns a two bedroom, two bath apartment
at 21 Alamitos Avenue in Long Beach which is located within 300
feet of the intersection of Alamitos Avenue and Ocean Boulevard.
You stated the councilmember was sole owner of the apartment, but
shared ownership in common areas of the complex in which the
apartment exists through a stock cooperative.  Councilmember
Drummond does not reside on the property, but rents it to a
tenant.  You stated there is a good likelihood that the project
will necessitate the city's purchase of the councilmember's
property.

     Councilmember Drummond has become concerned that his property
interest may preclude his participation in the consideration of
the revision of the transportation element of the city's general
plan, and other matters specifically implementing the grade
separation project at the intersection of Ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue.

                              ANALYSIS

Economic Interests

     Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.  A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) as
every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency and would include
a city councilmember.


Our File No. A-91-298
Page 3


     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

                    Section 87103(b) and (c).

     You have requested advice concerning potential conflicts of
interest resulting from Councilmember Drummond's interest in real
property and a tenant who leases the real property from the
councilmember.

     Section 87103(b) provides that an interest in real property
of $1,000 or more is a potentially disqualifying economic
interest. Moreover, any person² that has made a payment to the
councilmember in the past 12 months is a source of income to him
for the purposes of Section 87103(c). If the person has been a
source of income to the councilmember of $250 or more within the
12 months prior to a decision, that person is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest.

Foreseeability

     As stated above, Councilmember Drummond may not participate
in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his economic interest. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial


_________________________

2 Section 82047 provides "person" means an individual,
proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate,
business trust, company, corporation, association, committee, and
any other organization or group of persons acting in concert.

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 4


likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

The renovation of the intersection of Ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue would appear to have a foreseeable financial
effect on property near the intersection, as well as the tenant
living on the property. You stated that there is a good
likelihood that for the project to go forward, the councilmember's
property will need to be acquired by the city.

Moreover, it would appear that even absent the ultimate
acquisition of the councilmember's property, the improvement of
the intersection could foreseeably increase the fair market value
of property near the intersection. Conversely, increased traffic
may decrease the value of the property. While the nature and
extent of the effect is uncertain, some financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality

However, the foreseeable effect on the councilmember's
economic interest must also be material to require
disqualification. The Commission has adopted differing guidelines
to determine whether an effect is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision. The effect of a decision
on real property in which an official has a direct, indirect or
beneficial ownership interest, is material if:

The real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the official's
real property interests.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) (copy
enclosed).

You stated that the councilmember's real property is within
300 feet of the proposed grade separation project site. Since his
property is in such close proximity to the property which is the
subject of the decision, the councilmember may participate only if
there will be no financial effect on his real property. Thus, if
the decisions will have any financial effect on the fair market or
rental value of the real property of the councilmember, he must
disqualify from participating in the decision.

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 5


In addition to the financial effects on the councilmember's
real property, the councilmember must also disqualify himself if
the decision will have a material financial effect on his tenant
who is a source of income. According to your facts, the lessee is
not directly involved in any of the proceedings coming before the
city council concerning the revision to the transportation element
of the city's general plan. Consequently, the councilmember need
only look to the indirect financial effect on the lessee.

Regulation 18702.6 provides:

        The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

        (a) The decision will affect the
individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
or

        (b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under
Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

An interest in real property is defined to include any
leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to
acquire such an interest if the fair market value is $1,000 or
more, but excludes the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy
of one month or less. (Section 82033; Regulation 18233, copy
enclosed.) Consequently, if the tenant leases the property for
greater than a month-to-month basis, Councilmember Drumm must
also disqualify from the decision if there will be any effect on
the property interest of the tenant of the councilmember. In
addition, the councilmember must disqualify from any decision
which will affect the lessee's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities by $1,000 or more.

Other Decisions

        If you determine that the councilmember will have a conflict
of interest with respect to decisions concerning the grade
separation project, the city councilmember will also have to
disqualify with respect to all necessary decisions that precede
the actual grade separation project. For example, decisions
concerning funding of the work are inseparable from the actual
execution of the project with respect to which the official has a

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 6


conflict of interest. (Sweeney Advice Letter, No. A-89-639, copy
enclosed.) Moreover, if the decision is one that is critical to
the project and could result in termination or substantial
modification, the councilmember must also disqualify himself.
(Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454, copy enclosed.)

    Conversely, sometimes large and complex decisions may be
divided into separate decisions so that a public official with a
disqualifying interest as to one component of the decision may
participate in considering other components provided the other
components are not interrelated to the decision from which the
official was disqualified and the official has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed.)

    Where other subsequent decisions concerning the revision of
the transportation element of the city's general plan are
comparable for the grade separation project, or where decisions
concern property geographically remote from the site of the grade
separation project, the following procedure should be followed to
permit the councilmember to participate:

    (1) The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated from the
other decisions.

    (2) The decisions from which the councilmember is
disqualified should be considered first, and a final decision
reached by the city council without the councilmember
participating in any way.

    (3) Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
plan for which the councilmember has a disqualifying interest, the
councilmember may participate in the deliberations regarding other
portions of the plan, so long as those deliberations do not result
in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which he
was disqualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)

Exceptions

    1. The "Public Generally" Exception

    However, where an official has a financial interest that will
be financially affected by a governmental decision, the official
may still participate in the decision if the effect on the
official's interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703, copy
enclosed.) For the "public generally" exception to apply, the
decision must affect the councilmember's interests in

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 7


substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant
segment of the public in Long Beach. (In re Legan, (1985) 9 FPPC
Ops. 1, copy enclosed.)

Although we have not been provided with information on the
population distribution of Long Beach, it would not appear that
the segment of the population of Long Beach within 300 feet of the
site of the grade separation project at Ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue is significantly large to invoke the exception.

2.  Participation in the Official's Private Capacity

Regulation 18700.1(b) provides that an otherwise disqualified
official may appear in the same manner as any other member of the
general public before his or her agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function to represent his or her personal
interests in real property if the property is wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.

From the facts you have provided it appears that the
councilmember is the sole owner of the property in question, the
apartment. If this is the case, the councilmember may appear
before the city council in the same manner as any other member of
the public, to advocate on behalf of his property interest with
respect to the decision from which he was disqualified. However,
his comments must be limited to his personal interests, and the
councilmember should take care to clarify that he is not acting in
any official capacity. (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151, copy
enclosed.) The councilmember still would be prohibited from
privately discussing these matters with other members of the city
council or with other city officials.

Blind Trusts

You have asked whether placing the councilmember interest in
the real property in a trust would avoid conflicts of interest in
the future due to the property. Section 87103 provides that an
official has a pro-rata interest in any property or other interest
owned by a trust if the official, the official's agents, spouse,
and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
10-percent interest or greater. Thus, placing the councilmember's
property in a trust would not effect the disqualification
obligations of the councilmember.

However, Regulation 18235 (copy enclosed) provides that
property in a blind trust is not disqualifying. Regulation 18235

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 8


requires that for a blind trust to be valid it must comply with
the following:

     (1)  The trustee must be a disinterested party
other than the filer's spouse, child, parent,
grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-
in-law, brother-in-law, sister-in-law, aunt, uncle,
or first cousin or the spouse of any such person;

     (2)  The trustees must be given complete
discretion to manage the trust including, but not
limited to, the power to dispose of and acquire
trust assets without consulting or notifying the
filer;

     (3)  The trustee must be required to notify
the filer of the date of disposition and value at
disposition of any original investments or
interests in real property so that information can
be reported on the filer's next Statement of
Economic Interests;

     (4)  The trustee must be prohibited from
disclosing to the filer any information concerning
the replacement assets except for information
required under this subsection or the minimum tax
information which lists only the totals of taxable
items from the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual item of income; and

     (5)  If the trust is revoked while the filer
is a public official, or if the filer learns of any
replacement assets of the trust, the filer must
file an amendment to the most recent Statement of
Economic Interests disclosing the date of
revocation and the previously unreported pro rata
share of the trust's interests in real property or
investments or income deriving from any such
interests in real property or investments and
disqualify himself or herself, as necessary.  For
purposes of this regulation, any replacement assets
of which the filer learns shall thereafter be
treated as though they were original assets of the
trust.

     However, even the creation of a blind trust will not
immediately remedy the conflicts of interest that currently
confront the councilmember.  Original assets and any income
generated from those assets must continued to be disclosed until
they are disposed of by the trustee.  (Epstein Advice Letter, A-
84-224, copy enclosed.)

Our File No. A-91-298
Page 9


Thus, if the councilmember places his property interest in a
blind trust, he would still be disqualified from participating in
decisions that affect the property until the property is disposed
of by the trustee.³ (Dean Advice Letter, No. A-88-425, copy
enclosed.) Once the original assets are disposed of by the
trustee, the councilmember will no longer know or have reason to
know which financial interests are involved in a decision, and
therefore may participate without restriction. (Section 87100;
Biddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-403, copy enclosed.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosures


³ Moreover, if the councilmember knows who the purchaser of the
property is, the purchaser would be source of income and the
councilmember would continue to have a conflict of interest with
respect to decisions that affect the real property.

officiat

From the facts you have provided it appears that the

apartment. If this is the case, the councilmenber nay appear

However,
his comments nust be Iirnited to his oersonal interests, and the

any official 
capacity.

enllosed.) The councilnenber still

privately discussing these matters with other nenbers of the city
council or with other city officiaLs.
Blind Trusts
You have asked whether pl.acing the counc.ilnember interest in
the real proPerty in a trust would avoid confLicts of interest in
the future due to the property. Section 87103 provides that an
official 
has a pro-rata interlst in any property or other interest
owned by a trust if the of f ici.al, the official's 
agents, spouse,
and depindent children own dj.rectly, indirectly, or beneficially a
1O-per;ent interest or greater. Thus, placing the . counci Imember' s
property in a trust $/ould not effect the disqualification
obligations of the councilmernber.
However, Regulation 18235 (copy enclosed) provides that
property in a blind trust is not disqualj.fying. Regulation 18235

requires that for a blind trust to be valid it nust cornply with
the following:
(1) The trustee nust be a disinterested party
other than the filer's 
spouse, child, parent,
grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-
in-Iaw, brother- in- la$r, sister-in-Iaw, aunt, unc1e,
or first cousin or the spouse of any such person;
(2) The trustees must be given cornplete
discretion to rnanage the trust including, but not
Iimited to, the power to dispose of and acquire

(3) The trustee must be required to notify
the filer of the date of disposition and value at

interests in real property so that information can
be reported on the filer's 
next statement of
Econornic Interests;
(4) The trustee must be prohibited from
disclosing to the filer any information concerning
the replacetnent assets except for infor:nation
required under this subsection or the ninimum tax

items frolB the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual iten of incone,' and
(5) If the trust is revoked while the filer
is a public official, 
or if the filer learns of any
replacement assets of the trust, the filer 
must
file an amendnent to the nost recent Statenent of
Econornic Interests disclosing the date of
revocation and the previously unreported Pro rata
share of the trust's interests in real proPerty or
investnents or income deriving fron any such
interests in real property or investments and
disqualify himself or herself, as necessary. For
purposes of this regulation, any replacement assets
of which the filer learns shall. thereafter be
treated as though they were original assets of the
trust.
Ho$rever, even the creation of a bLind trust will not
irnmediately renedy the conflicts of interest that currentLy
confront the councilnernber. original assets and any income

fhey are disposed of by the trustee.

embedding.first_500_words: July 8, 1991 John R. Calhoun Long Beach City Attorney Office of City Attorney City Hall 333 West Ocean Boulevard Long Beach, CA 90802 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-91-298 Dear Mr. Calhoun: This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of Long Beach City Councilmember Douglas S. Drummond regarding the councilmember's responsibilities under the conflict- of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION May Councilmember Drummond participate in Long Beach City Council consideration of a revision to the transportation element of the city's general plan which would include a grade separation project slated for an intersection within 300 feet of property owned and leased out by Councilmember Drummond? CONCLUSION Councilmember Drummond may not participate in any decisions concerning the grade separation project if the decision will have any financial effect on the fair market or rental value of the real property of the councilmember. The councilmember will also have to disqualify with respect to all decisions concerning the revision of the transportation element of the city's general plan which are critical to the project as a whole and could result in termination or substantial modification of the grade separation project. However, Councilmember Drummond may participate in _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-91-298 Page 2 considering other components of the revision of the transportation element of the city's general plan which will not affect the grade separation project, provided the councilmember has no other disqualifying interest with respect to the other components. FACTS The Long Beach City Council will be considering a comprehensive revision of the transportation element of the city's general plan. The proposed revised element applies traffic circulation within the entire city and includes over 830 miles of streets and approximately 9,000 intersections. The City of Long Beach has a population of approximately 430,000 persons and covers a geographic area of roughly 50 square miles. In the revision of the element, the intersection of Ocean Boulevard and Alamitos Avenue has been slated for a grade separation project. You stated the project would reconstruct the streets to intersect on two different levels. You also stated the project may require the purchase of real property around the intersection. Councilmember Drummond owns a two bedroom, two bath apartment at 21 Alamitos Avenue in Long Beach which is located within 300 feet of the intersection of Alamitos Avenue and Ocean Boulevard. You stated the councilmember was sole owner of the apartment, but shared ownership in common areas of the complex in which the apartment exists through a stock cooperative. Councilmember Drummond does not reside on the property, but rents it to a tenant. You
